Required Insurance (Platform-Level): TLC commercial auto per vehicle ($100K/$300K liability; $100K PIP per person from March 2026)
Required Insurance (Driver-Level): TLC hack license required; individual TLC commercial auto policy per vehicle (cannot pool at platform level)
Discretionary Insurance: Umbrella excess $5M+; cyber liability; D&O; E&O
Liability Framework: TLC per-vehicle filing; platform named as co-defendant in most claims; driver bears primary insurance obligation per-vehicle
Platform Coop Gray Zone?: Medium — cooperative model not tested under TLC FHV framework; TLC base license required; FHV vehicle cap (2018) limits new entrants
Cooperative Rideshare Precedent: Drivers Cooperative NYC (active 2021) — using per-vehicle TLC insurance; no platform pooling confirmed
Notes: Insurance market in crisis: American Transit Insurance Co. insolvency (2025) triggers 25% rate increase through 2028. TLC per-vehicle structure prevents cooperative pooling. FHV cap blocks new vehicle licenses. Most expensive and most structurally hostile market. Return in Phase 4+

Required Insurance (Platform-Level): CPUC requires: Period 1: $50K/$100K/$30K; Periods 2/3: $1M CSL + $1M UM/UIM; excess $200K/occurrence; occupational accident insurance (Prop 22: $1M medical + 66% disability earnings replacement)
Required Insurance (Driver-Level): Driver personal policy must have TNC endorsement or commercial auto; Prop 22 occupational accident insurance mandatory
Discretionary Insurance: Umbrella excess $5M+; cyber liability; D&O; E&O; cargo if delivery
Liability Framework: Prop 22 (upheld CA Supreme Court July 2024) = driver is independent contractor but platform provides occupational accident coverage; platform faces vicarious liability claims; driver primary for personal negligence
Platform Coop Gray Zone?: Low — CPUC TNC permit process is well-established; Prop 22 creates a cooperative-neutral framework; occupational accident insurance requirement applies equally to cooperative and corporate platform
Annual Platform Fee (est.): CPUC TCP permit (~$100–$500 initial; 0.33% gross revenue annual assessment)
Cooperative Rideshare Precedent: Fare Co-op operates in California (active); LA-based driver co-ops have operated historically
Notes: Strong cooperative movement; progressive regulatory environment. Expensive market for insurance and operations. Prop 22 creates occupational accident insurance obligation that increases per-driver cost but aligns with cooperative values. CPUC reporting requirements are extensive.

Required Insurance (Platform-Level): Portland: Period 1: $50K/$100K/$25K; Periods 2/3: $1M CSL + $1M UM/UIM; platform must hold coverage
Required Insurance (Driver-Level): Driver personal policy must have TNC endorsement; platform policy covers Periods 2/3
Discretionary Insurance: Occupational accident (voluntary but recommended); umbrella excess; cyber liability; D&O
Liability Framework: Platform carries Period 2/3 commercial auto; driver personal for Period 1; Portland PBOT named in regulatory disputes; clear claim hierarchy once platform policy is established
Platform Coop Gray Zone?: Low — Portland PBOT has already approved Fare Co-op (a cooperative); no additional cooperative-specific requirements; city treats cooperative same as corporate TNC
Regulatory Authority: Portland Bureau of Transportation (PBOT); Oregon DOT
Annual Platform Fee (est.): Portland PBOT private for-hire permit (~$5K–$15K estimate; VERIFY current fee)
Cooperative Rideshare Precedent: Fare Co-op operating in Portland (approved by PBOT); precedent established
Notes: Highest cooperative regulatory familiarity in US. PBOT has already approved a cooperative platform. Oregon requires strong UM/UIM ($1M) adding slight cost vs. basic states but still manageable. Progressive city with strong cooperative and labor organizing culture. Y-Risk policy covers Oregon.

State Preemption?: Partial (UTC statewide; Seattle has significant add-ons)
Required Insurance (Platform-Level): Washington: Period 1: $50K/$100K/$30K; Periods 2/3: $1M CSL; workers' compensation required for TNC drivers (unique to WA); paid sick leave accrual required
Required Insurance (Driver-Level): Driver personal auto with TNC endorsement; workers' comp covered at platform level (WA mandate)
Discretionary Insurance: Occupational accident already mandated; umbrella excess; cyber liability; D&O
Liability Framework: WA mandates TNC platforms provide workers' comp coverage — this is the only US state with this requirement; platform bears workers' comp liability as if drivers are employees for insurance purposes; minimum per-mile and per-minute pay rates set by L&I adjusted annually
Platform Coop Gray Zone?: Low — UTC and Seattle have clear TNC frameworks; cooperative structure is not addressed specifically but TNC law is carrier-neutral; workers' comp mandate affects cost but aligns with cooperative values
Regulatory Authority: Washington UTC (statewide); Seattle Office of Labor Standards (additional local requirements)
Annual Platform Fee (est.): UTC TNC permit (~$5K–$20K estimate; VERIFY) + Seattle annual renewal
Cooperative Rideshare Precedent: No specific cooperative rideshare precedent in Seattle; Seattle Solidarity Mutual Aid and labor organizing history is supportive
Notes: Workers' comp mandate (unique to WA) increases cost but is ethically aligned with cooperative model. Seattle minimum pay rates are protective for drivers. Strong driver organizing culture. Labor progressive environment likely receptive to cooperative platform.

State Preemption?: Partial (PUC statewide; Denver has some add-ons)
Required Insurance (Platform-Level): Colorado: Period 1: $50K/$100K/$30K; Periods 2/3: $1M CSL; UM/UIM $200K/$400K required (higher than most states); PUC permit required
Required Insurance (Driver-Level): Driver personal auto with TNC endorsement; platform provides Period 2/3 coverage
Discretionary Insurance: Occupational accident (voluntary recommended); umbrella excess; cyber liability; D&O
Liability Framework: Platform carries primary Period 2/3; UM/UIM higher requirement means driver injury better covered; standard negligence applies for accident fault
Platform Coop Gray Zone?: Low — Drivers Cooperative Colorado is live; PUC has issued TNC permits to cooperative entity; Colorado law is cooperative-neutral; platform cooperative registered as LCA (Limited Cooperative Association)
Annual Platform Fee (est.): $111,250 large TNC annual fee (VERIFY — small/startup tier may be lower for cooperatives with fewer than X drivers; DCC may have obtained reduced-fee status)
Cooperative Rideshare Precedent: Drivers Cooperative Colorado (active fall 2024) — operating in Denver, Colorado Springs, Fort Collins, Vail; 4600+ drivers enrolled
Notes: Live proof-of-concept. DCC documentation shows 5-month insurance search and $500K minimum capital requirement — critical planning constraint. UM/UIM $200K/$400K requirement slightly higher than basic states. Large platform annual fee may have startup tier — verify. Strong cooperative development infrastructure (RMEOC).

State Preemption?: Partial (Illinois Commerce Commission statewide; Chicago has significant add-ons)
Required Insurance (Platform-Level): Illinois: Period 1: $50K/$100K/$25K; Periods 2/3: $1M CSL; Chicago: additional chauffeur's license; city per-trip fee ($3/ride downtown); annual city TNC license
Required Insurance (Driver-Level): Driver: Illinois personal auto with TNC endorsement; chauffeur's license (city requirement adds onboarding friction); platform covers Periods 2/3
Discretionary Insurance: Occupational accident (voluntary); umbrella excess; cyber liability; D&O
Liability Framework: Platform carries Period 2/3; city chauffeur license creates driver accountability layer; per-trip fee reduces net revenue; standard negligence for accident fault
Platform Coop Gray Zone?: Medium — Chicago's chauffeur's license requirement and per-trip fee structure were designed for traditional FHV operators; cooperative governance structure not specifically addressed in Chicago TNP ordinance; legal gray zone around driver-member onboarding under chauffeur's license rules
Regulatory Authority: Illinois Commerce Commission (statewide); Chicago BACP (city license)
Annual Platform Fee (est.): $10,000 Chicago TNP annual license + Illinois Commerce Commission fee; $3/trip city fee
Cooperative Rideshare Precedent: No cooperative rideshare precedent in Chicago specifically; The Drivers Cooperative NYC has Chicago drivers in its network informally
Notes: Per-trip fee ($3/downtown) reduces driver and rider economics significantly. Chauffeur's license adds driver onboarding cost and time. Insurance market manageable. Strong labor organizing history. South Side and immigrant driver communities may be receptive to cooperative model.

State Preemption?: Strong (statewide preemption — cities cannot add requirements)
Required Insurance (Platform-Level): Georgia: Period 1: $50K/$100K/$25K; Periods 2/3: $1M CSL; UM/UIM $100K/$300K during passenger transport
Required Insurance (Driver-Level): Driver personal auto with TNC endorsement; platform carries Period 2/3 commercial auto
Discretionary Insurance: Occupational accident (voluntary recommended); umbrella excess; cyber liability; D&O
Liability Framework: Standard three-period framework; platform carries Period 2/3; strong preemption means no city-level liability surprises; standard negligence for accident fault
Platform Coop Gray Zone?: Low — Georgia TNC law is statewide and carrier-neutral; cooperative structure treated identically to corporate TNC under state law; Fare Co-op has Georgia authority letter on file; no cooperative-specific regulatory ambiguity
Regulatory Authority: Georgia Public Service Commission; Georgia DOR
Annual Platform Fee (est.): ~$75,000 annual state fee (VERIFY — this is historical Uber/Lyft fee; small operator tier may be lower)
Cooperative Rideshare Precedent: Fare Co-op has Georgia authority letter and is operating; established cooperative precedent
Launch Recommendation: Recommended for Phase 2 — Best First Market
Notes: Lowest insurance cost market among targets. Statewide preemption eliminates city-level compliance complexity. Fare Co-op presence confirms cooperative platforms are accepted. No fingerprint requirement at state level. Diverse driver population (large immigrant communities) aligned with cooperative mission. Verify whether startup-tier fee exists below $75K.

State Preemption?: Strong (HB 100 2017 — statewide preemption; Austin fought and lost)
Required Insurance (Platform-Level): Texas: Period 1: $50K/$100K/$25K (VERIFY exact TX minimums from TDLR); Periods 2/3: $1M CSL
Required Insurance (Driver-Level): Driver personal auto with TNC endorsement; platform carries Period 2/3
Discretionary Insurance: Occupational accident (voluntary); umbrella excess; cyber liability; D&O
Liability Framework: Strong statewide preemption; platform carries Period 2/3; standard negligence; no city-level fingerprint requirement (Austin had fingerprints; HB 100 removed that)
Platform Coop Gray Zone?: Low — Texas TNC law is explicitly carrier-neutral; statewide preemption removes any city-level cooperative scrutiny; TDLR administers; no cooperative-specific regulation
Regulatory Authority: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation (TDLR)
Annual Platform Fee (est.): $5,000 annual state fee (among lowest in US)
Cooperative Rideshare Precedent: No cooperative rideshare precedent in Austin specifically; Texas has small cooperative sector
Notes: Lowest annual state fee ($5,000). Lowest insurance cost estimates. Statewide preemption means one license, one regulatory relationship. No fingerprint requirement. Gig economy is large in Austin/Dallas/Houston. Conservative political environment may be less cooperative-friendly for organizing, but regulatory barriers are lowest in the US.
